Let's keep going. Bethany, questions? Yes, we have some from this morning that I would love to touch on. Tim Turner asked way back when we were going on one-liners. Can the one-liner be two sentences? Are we supposed to chop it down to one sentence? It can be one sentence, two sentences, or three sentences. It doesn't matter. I'm not going to argue against your punctuation. You do that however you want. A one-liner is really one statement. Yeah. It's a better way to say it, but one-liner sounds cooler. It does. Sounds really cool. Better than a one statement. Eaton asks, what if you have multiple avatars that want completely different things? Would you just then focus on a specific avatar on the home page? It works on digital marketers' home page because all the avatars are trying to outmarket their competitors, but if they want completely different things, how does that work together? At some point, I'm not a huge fan of a company being known for more than one thing. At some point, you just create a different brand. You really do. Dave Ramsey can get everything under financial peace because he's working with entrepreneurs. He's working with female entrepreneurs. He's working with financial advisors. He's working with real estate agents, looking for mortgage. He's working with people who sell life insurance. Those are the ones I can think about the top of my head, but all of them have to do with financial peace. He can get away with that. However, let's say you have a plumber who comes to your house and he fixes your toilet and you say, man, you did a fantastic. You were on time and you were under budget. It's rare to find a plumber that does that. The plumber turns around and says, thank you so much. I'd love for you to know about my other business. I'm an at-home chef. I can come and fix your toilet and make your dinner at the same time. Not going to work. Those brands are incompatible with each other. The trick is to know that our friend who's the chef plumber needs to separate those identities and probably not let anybody know that it's the same guy. It's just too confusing and too much. You've got to be able to into it when we need to separate and create two brands. I love that. I think we had a couple other questions that were similar around just not overwhelming your visitor on your homepage. If we have multiple lead magnets or lead generators that are playing around on that homepage, what are your thoughts on? There's all sorts of digital market. The content inside of the platform that you're a part of is going to have a lot about if somebody has downloaded this email, they're probably a real estate agent. When they come to the homepage, their homepage is going to look different. That's not what we do. We do messaging. You'll want to be able to look into that stuff. My company is five, six years old now. We're just now starting to target different demographics. Probably for most of us, there's a lot of work to do before we get there. I would say the more that that message can feel personal to the person who's reading it, the better you're going to do. If there's ways of reaching out to different people on the homepage, that's great. I do think the homepage, there needs to be a controlling idea behind the homepage. The controlling idea is going to reach out to somebody specific. Awesome. Karen asks, can the call to action be to book a call? Yes. Especially if there's a long runway on selling a product and we need to schedule a call about knee surgery, you're not going to click buy knee surgery now. That's not going to happen. Send me the knees. I'll do it myself. That's book a call, schedule a consultation. That's in reality, that is the direct call to action. That leads people into the physical in-person pipeline that gets them through the process. I would consider that a direct call to action. Great. And Hommar asks, should we also incorporate these sections? I think we were in the building yourself as a guide section. Should we incorporate that into the homepage as well? The guide? The language that positions you as a guide? Yes. 100%. That needs to be on your homepage. And the header of your homepage should also pass the grunt test. There's no excuse for not passing the grunt test, whether it's a landing page or homepage. And you absolutely 100% want to position yourself as the guide. That's got to happen on the homepage. Awesome. Manny asks, when going into a new niche, how can you showcase competency without being too generic? Huh. How can you... I mean, to me, I'd have to see the collateral that you're talking about and tell you whether or not you're being too generic. I would say you can't be... you can't be generic. You've... it's really got to be specific. But that's a very contextual question. I'm not exactly sure what you mean. I will say showcasing competency, though, in this train of thought, and I said this earlier in the workshop, is much easier than you thought. You're all going to feel really insecure about why people should trust you. They're literally just looking for one little statement that gives them the excuse to trust you. And, in fact, it's easier to overdo a demonstration of competency than under demonstrate competency. You really just need... you can tell one story about one client who you helped and your customers are going to check it off their list and say, okay, this person knows what they're doing. But yeah, hopefully that helped a little bit. Okay. Deb asks, can you quantify less tangible products or services like meditation where the desired end result is peace or less stress or something like that? How would you frame that? I mean, how do you make that feel more specific is probably what she's asking. Well, you know, what does peace and meditation give you? It gives you better relationships with other people. It gives you a more centered, balanced approach to your work, which makes you more money. I mean, you know, just start listing what you get with meditation besides just peace. And you're going to come up with some really tangible things that people would invest in in a short period of time. And all those things, of course, need to help us survive. Yeah, absolutely. Ashley asks, where do you add a gallery for a portfolio, for example? Does that go in the junk drawer? As a photographer, my work is very visual. Also taking SEO into consideration. There's not a lot of content on the page. So how do you really get found organically with that approach? Yeah, when you're talking about something that people need to see, even like home buying and those sorts of things, you probably, you know, there's not a specific place that you can put it, but you probably want to get it up somewhere near the top. So if I were a photographer, and let's say I were a wedding photographer, I would still do a header. I would do looping images, looping video of beautiful stuff that I've shot. So that visually is showing it. And then number two, I would still do stakes. And then number three, I would do the portfolio. Right? I would still do the header and the stakes because that hooks them into the story. And then I would get the portfolio and go on down with guide and all that stuff after that. But yeah, just stick that maybe number three. Awesome. And actually, I think I'm going to skip this question because we kind of covered that with Eaton's question. Tim asks, didn't see any client testimonials in the website formula? Is that important to sprinkle in? There is client testimonials in the website formula that's not on our sample. So 100%. If you read building a story brand, testimonials are one of the ways you demonstrate competency. A couple words. I'm glad you said something about testimonials. I'm going to say something that's really controversial. And then you let me paraphrase a little bit. I want you to write your customers testimonials for them because they aren't writers. And so when somebody says to you, wow, I thought that was going to be way more expensive, that should cue you to say, actually, can I just write that down? I thought Acme Feed-n-Seed was going to be way more expensive. And can I just sign your name to that? You're getting their approval. But these long testimonials, you know, I walked into Acme Feed-n-Seed and I just couldn't believe how nice Jake was. In fact, Jake dated my daughter for a little bit, so I was kind of mad at him. But you know, he's like, what does this have to do with the business? So, you know, you want to get to that point where you're in great testimonials. One of the things you want a testimony that does, you want to, at least one of the testimonies that you use should overcome the main resistance that people have to buying from you. All right? So the main resistance people have for Storybrand is really surprising to me. They say this, it probably won't work for me. I'm a dentist. You know, fill in whatever. It won't work for me because I'm a real estate agent. It won't work for me because we have a grocery store. It won't work for me because we're business to business. It won't work for me because we're business to consumer. It won't work for me because we work with the government. It won't work for me. We just hear it constantly. And so one of the testimonies that we would want to take is, you know, I discovered that anybody who uses words to sell things will benefit from Storybrand. And if I can put that overcoming that resistance into the mouth of my customer, it's a lot more powerful. So why, what is the main reason people aren't buying from you and have a customer testimony will overcome that? All right? Yeah, excellent. I've got a couple more and we'll go. And then we'll look at websites. Okay, couple more and we'll look at websites. Sounds good. Lena asks, what is your one piece of advice to someone who is just starting his business on how to use messages from the brand script to kickstart the business and start making money? So basically what would I do first in terms of kickstarting the business? Yes. Okay, well, let me tell you what I wouldn't do. I wouldn't do what most people do. And that is they start figuring out their branding. That is their logo, their colors, all that kind of stuff. I have a friend who who started a company with venture capital money. This always happens with venture capital money, right? You get she's fresh out of Oxford. She's very smart, gets venture capital money, and she starts figuring out the logo, the name of the company, all the branding stuff. She can only do that because she's got venture capital money. In other words, she's burning money before even selling the product. What I would do is I would identify the problem that that product solves. And I would start telling people about it. And I don't care if you're having to sell it out of your trunk, right? This is what I would do if I were venture capital. And if somebody came to me and wanted to start a company, I said, okay, I'm going to pay your salary. I will not pay for anything else. Everything else has to come from sales the company generates. And what that does is it forces the person to actually burn to build a real company. Venture capital, we work is not a real company. We work is a company, a money pit that people have been pouring into for a very long time. And everybody's going to lose their shorts. We work looks like a real company. Real companies make money. Right? So what I would do in order to make money, identify the problem you solve, start telling people that you saw that problem, start selling that product, then capture email addresses so you can tell more people, then hire sales reps so you can tell more people, so on and so on. I'm filming a course in December called business strategy made simple. And business strategy made simple, you'll be the first to hear it outside of my community, is basically how I grew my company. And the way I grew my company was like this. I saw the company as an airplane. An airplane. The body of the company was the overhead. The wings were the product. The right engine was marketing. The left engine was sales. If you build a company that has huge overhead and tiny little wings that aren't profitable and little bit of engines, that company is going to crash. So you cannot add overhead unless you put money and fuel into the right engine and the left engine and that lifts the products. And they all have to be in proportion. If I go to the airport tomorrow morning to get on an airplane and the body of that airplane is enormous and it's got two little propellers, I'm not getting on that airplane. So when I go to a company and I go, oh, I see you've got a lot of swag with your logo on it and you've got 25 employees in a really nice office and you're not selling anything yet. What am I thinking? Do not invest in this company. This company is going down. So what would I do if I were first starting out? I'd build a damn good airplane with extremely powerful engines, very big wings and a tiny little body. I'd build it like a rocket and then I'd slowly add overhead over time. Slowly, sheepishly, fearfully do you add overhead. Make sure the rest of your money. And by the way, fuel is cash flow. You can have a beautiful airplane. If you run out of cash, it's going to crash. So you always want to monitor your fuel going into those right and left engines. Okay. All right. Last question. Can you tell us again about the book you mentioned earlier for preorder that we could email and receive? If you make me, yeah. I mean, if I have to. So how to bring it up. All right. So the book is business made simple. You guys want to hear, here's my pitch. It's wrong that you spent money on a college education and still don't know how to grow a business. It's wrong. Jazz hands, if you feel that way about college education, your degree, how many of you could not buy a house until you were in your 30s because you were paying off Duke? Let me tell you something. Duke cannot charge their students tuition for 100 years before they run out of money. That's how much money they have. Right? Yeah, exactly. So business made simple is 60 daily readings. When you get the book, you'll send an email to me and we spent north of $200,000 filming 60 videos that go with this book. This is an MBA for $20. It's an MBA for 20 bucks. And if you get it now, like you can preorder it on Amazon, Barnes & Noble, wherever you buy books. When you get that receipt forward your email to book at businessmadesimple.com. Book at businessmadesimple.com and you're going to get an email and that email is going to say, we have a course called zero to 10 million that explains how Donald Miller built a $10 million company in about five years. And that course is being edited right now. The second that course is available, I'm going to send it to you. So you'll get that in the next couple of weeks. That course, the book comes out in January 19th. It's going to be like giving yourself a Christmas gift when you get that book. So business made simple, grab that book today. Here's the other thing. If you have a company and you have a bunch of employees and you want them to spend 60 days thinking about how to make you more money, get a copy for every single one of your employees. Because that's what they're going to do. They're going to spend 60 days thinking about how to make you more money and also grow their careers. By the way, I tell them in the book, if you know how to do these 60 things, your boss should pay you more money. So be careful. It's going to bite you. But you can increase that overhead if they're making you more money, can't you? I mean, you owe them. You got to give them some of that money.
